hero-title: What AI is really good at — and where it slips up
What AI's good at — and where it slips up
What AI's great at — and where it trips up
What AI's good at — and where it slips
hero-lede: The most useful thing to know is not a list of tricks. It is a feel for when you can let the AI do a lot, and when you should stay in charge. This page shows that feel in one picture. Then, at the bottom, you can try it on a task of your own.

The most useful thing to carry isn't a list of tricks — it's a feel for when to lean on the AI and when to keep a hand on the wheel. Here's that feel at a glance. Then try rating your own task at the bottom.

The single most useful thing to carry around isn't a list of tricks — it's a feel for when to lean in and when to keep a hand on the wheel. Here it is at a glance. Then rate your own task at the bottom.
The useful thing isn't a trick list — it's a feel for when to lean in and when to keep a hand on the wheel. Here it is at a glance; rate your own task below.
Not tricks — a feel for when to lean in and when to steer. At a glance, then rate your own task.
rate-h: How much can the AI help with your task?

rate-sub: Tick each line that is true for the task you have right now. The meter gives a rough guide, not an order — the final choice is always yours.
Tick whatever's true of the task in front of you. The meter is a rule of thumb, not a ruling — the call is always yours.
Tick whatever's true about the task in front of you. The meter's a rule of thumb, not a ruling — you always make the call.
Tick what's true of your task. The meter's a rule of thumb, not a ruling — the call stays yours.
Tick what's true. Rule of thumb, not ruling — your call.
fig-note: The numbers above and the meter are rough guides, not exact measurements — they give you a feel for the shape of things, not a promise. The true answer also depends on your exact task, how well you steer the tool, and whether it knows when to step back. You are the one who decides; the meter only gives a nudge.

Those figures and the meter are rough guides, not measurements — a feel for the shape, not a promise. The real answer also depends on the exact task, how well you steer, and whether the tool knows when to back off. You decide; the meter just nudges.

Those figures and the meter are rules of thumb, not measurements — a feel for the shape, not a promise. The real answer also depends on the exact task, how well you steer the tool, and whether it knows when to back off. You're the one who decides; the meter just nudges.

Figures and meter are rules of thumb, not measurements — shape, not promise. The real answer depends on the task, your steering, and whether the tool knows when to back off. You decide; the meter nudges.
Rules of thumb, not measurements. The answer depends on the task, your steering, and the tool's restraint. You decide.
next-p: The real skill here is called calibration — working out how far to trust the tool on this task, the one in front of you. That is a human skill, and it gets better with practice.
The whole knack is calibration — working out how far to trust it on this task. That's a human skill, and it sharpens with practice.
The whole knack is calibration — knowing how far to trust it on this one. That's a human skill worth sharpening.
The knack is calibration — how far to trust it on this one. A human skill worth sharpening.
Calibration: how far to trust it on this one. A skill worth sharpening.
